event: The Saqqaq culture, Greenland's earliest known inhabitants, arrived from Arctic Canada and settled along the coasts.
The Dorset culture (Early Dorset) began establishing itself in Greenland as the Saqqaq presence declined.
The Independence I culture occupied the far northern regions of Greenland during the early Paleo-Eskimo period.
The Independence II culture inhabited northern Greenland following earlier Paleo-Eskimo groups.
Norse explorer Erik the Red led settlers from Iceland to establish the Eastern and Western Settlements, naming the land Greenland.
Leif Erikson sailed from Greenland and reached Vinland in North America, marking the first known European contact with the continent.
The first bishop of Greenland was appointed, and the cathedral at Gardar became the seat of the Norse Christian church.
The Thule people, ancestors of modern Inuit, migrated into Greenland from Arctic Canada, eventually displacing the Dorset culture.
The Norse settlers of Greenland accepted the sovereignty of the Norwegian crown.
A wedding recorded at Hvalsey Church became the last documented event of the Norse Greenlanders before their settlements vanished.
The Norse Greenland settlements had been completely abandoned, leaving the Inuit as the island's sole inhabitants.
Dano-Norwegian missionary Hans Egede founded a colony near present-day Nuuk, beginning renewed European colonization.
The settlement of Godthaab, the future capital Nuuk, was established as a permanent colonial base.
Denmark established the Royal Greenland Trading Department, granting it a monopoly over commerce and isolating Greenland from outside trade.
Under the Treaty of Kiel, Norway was ceded to Sweden while Greenland remained under sole Danish control.
Fridtjof Nansen led the first successful crossing of the Greenland ice sheet, advancing polar exploration.
American explorer Robert Peary used northern Greenland as a base for his expeditions toward the North Pole.
Two regional councils (landsraad) were established in North and South Greenland, giving Inuit limited local representation.
Norway claimed part of eastern Greenland as Erik the Red's Land, sparking a territorial dispute with Denmark.
The Permanent Court of International Justice ruled in Denmark's favor, confirming Danish sovereignty over all of Greenland.
After Nazi Germany occupied Denmark, Greenland was effectively cut off and came under American protection during World War II.
The United States established military bases and weather stations in Greenland under a defense agreement signed by the Danish ambassador.
Denmark and the United States signed a defense treaty allowing the U.S. to maintain bases, including the major facility at Thule.
Greenland's colonial status was abolished and it became an integral county of Denmark with seats in the Danish parliament.
Inuit families at Thule were forcibly relocated to make way for the expansion of the American air base.
A U.S. B-52 bomber carrying nuclear weapons crashed near Thule, contaminating the area and causing a diplomatic crisis.
Greenland voted against joining the European Economic Community, though Denmark's membership brought it in regardless.
Greenland achieved Home Rule, gaining its own parliament and government with control over many domestic affairs.
Greenland formally withdrew from the European Economic Community, the first territory ever to leave the bloc.
Investigations revealed extensive secret U.S. nuclear activities at Thule, prompting compensation claims from relocated Inuit.
The Danish Supreme Court awarded compensation to the Thule community for their 1953 forced relocation.
Greenlanders voted overwhelmingly in favor of expanded self-government in a landmark referendum.
The Self-Government Act took effect, recognizing Greenlanders as a distinct people and granting greater autonomy and resource rights.
Kalaallisut (Greenlandic) became the sole official language of Greenland under the new self-government arrangement.
Greenland's parliament lifted a decades-old ban on uranium mining, opening the door to controversial mineral extraction.
U.S. President Donald Trump publicly proposed buying Greenland from Denmark, an offer firmly rejected by Danish and Greenlandic leaders.
The Inuit Ataqatigiit party won elections partly by opposing the Kvanefjeld rare-earth and uranium mining project.
Greenland's parliament passed a law banning uranium mining and exploration, halting major rare-earth projects.
Greenland presented a draft constitution as part of ongoing debate over a future path toward full independence from Denmark.
Renewed U.S. interest in acquiring Greenland intensified geopolitical attention on the island and its strategic Arctic position.
